text: The surgeon invaginates the original layer to ensure complete closure, preferring a two-layer closure technique. The resection begins with a medial to lateral approach, dissecting the retroperitoneal plane easily due to pelvic inflammation. Posterior to the rectum, the surgeon identifies mesh tacked into the sacrum, dividing it flush with the bone to avoid significant bleeding. The medial dissection continues as the surgeon searches for the ureter, noting the absence of urethral catheters.

The surgeon invaginates the original layer to ensure complete closure, preferring a two-layer closure technique. The resection begins with a medial to lateral approach, revealing the retroperitoneal plane. The surgeon dissects posterior to the rectum, identifying and dividing the mesh anchored to the sacrum in the retrorectal space. A large tack is visible holding the mesh, which is carefully divided flush with the bone to avoid significant bleeding. The surgeon continues the medial dissection, searching for the ureter without the aid of stents.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, identifying the medial plane where a sponge is located, and proceeds down the left pelvic sidewall. The sigmoid colon is targeted for dissection, with the bladder visibly draped over it. Due to the patient's prior hysterectomy, the uterus is absent, creating a clear surgical field. The surgeon carefully dissects around the serosa of the colon to avoid injuring the bladder, using laparoscopic suction for assistance. The dissection progresses circumferentially, and the surgeon enters the vagina without encountering the mesh yet.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection along the left pelvic sidewall, searching for the medial plane where a sponge is located. The sigmoid colon is being dissected, with the bladder visible draped over it. The absence of a uterus, due to a previous hysterectomy, provides a clear view. The surgeon carefully navigates around the serosa of the colon to avoid injuring the bladder amidst scarring and blood.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic suction and retraction to assist in the dissection. The vagina is entered, and bleeding is cauterized to ensure safety. Posterior dissection elevates the rectum, revealing lateral attachments. The right ureter is dissected for identification. The surgeon enters the deep pelvic space, glimpses the rectum, and removes mesh from the vagina's back wall, including Ethabon sutures. Adhesions are taken down, completing the pelvic dissection and fistula removal, with the mesh excised en bloc with the colon.

The surgeon cauterizes a bleeding spot to ensure a clear surgical field, then continues posterior dissection to elevate the rectum from the pelvis, revealing lateral attachments. The right ureter is dissected for easier identification. The surgeon enters the deep pelvic space, glimpses the rectum, and removes mesh from the vagina's back wall, ensuring all mesh and Ethabon sutures are extracted. The pelvic dissection concludes with the fistula taken down and mesh removed en bloc with the colon, followed by further mobilization to facilitate anastomosis.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon to ensure a tension-free anastomosis, preserving the sigmoid branches without high ligation of the superior hemorrhoidal or inferior mesenteric arteries. The proximal colon mesentery is cleared, and the distal margin is prepared using the vessel sealer. Edematous bowel is observed, but deemed suitable for anastomosis. ICG is used to confirm blood supply before dividing the proximal and distal margins. The specimen is extracted through the vagina, including the small bowel specimen.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon without needing to mobilize the flexure, aiming for a tension-free anastomosis. The mesentery to the proximal colon is taken while preserving sigmoid branches, avoiding high ligation of major arteries. The proximal and distal margins are cleared, using mesh as a handle, and the vessel sealer is applied to the rectum, which shows edema but is otherwise healthy. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to confirm blood supply before dividing the proximal margin. The specimen is extracted through the vagina, including the small bowel specimen.

The surgeon closes the enlarged vaginal defect, ensuring complete excision of mesh and fresh edges for closure. The bladder is visibly prolapsing into the anterior proximal vagina, prompting careful, superficial suturing to avoid catching the bladder or ureter orifices. The left side of the vaginal opening is closed, and the suture is run back to the right. The setup for a hand-sewn colorectal anastomosis follows, with the surgeon suturing the staple lines of the proximal and distal margins together as a back outer row.

The surgeon closes the vaginal defect, which was enlarged to excise all mesh and obtain fresh edges for closure. The bladder is visibly prolapsing into the anterior proximal vagina, requiring careful superficial suturing to avoid catching the bladder or ureter orifices. The surgeon completes the closure of the left side of the vaginal opening and runs the suture back to the right. Following the vaginal closure, the setup for a hand-sewn colorectal anastomosis is prepared, with the surgeon suturing the staple lines of the proximal and distal margins together.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn colorectal anastomosis, starting with a proctotomy and collotomy. The thickened bowel walls due to secondary inflammation are visible. The surgeon removes stool puckering and continues suturing, beginning in the middle and sewing out to the corners. The inner lips of the rectum and descending colon are sewn, ensuring they do not become ischemic. The surgeon completes the anastomosis with a two-layer technique, feeling secure with the back row and inverting the anterior row.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn colorectal anastomosis, beginning with a proctotomy that reveals thickened tissue due to secondary inflammation. The surgeon removes stool puckering before continuing with the collotomy. The inner row of sutures is started in the middle and sewn out to the far corner on the left-hand side, ensuring the inner lips of the rectum and descending colon do not become ischemic. The surgeon uses ICG to confirm blood supply and proceeds with a two-layer anastomosis, sewing the posterior suture out and around anteriorly.

The surgeon visualizes the common aneurotomy and ensures complete closure by tying sutures across it. The posterior suture from the outer row is extended around as an outer anterior row to imbricate the anastomosis. A proctoscopy confirms the anastomotic lumen is tight, allowing passage of a 19mm proctoscope without obstruction. A leak test is performed, and pericolic fat is placed over the exposed bone portion. Omentum is brought down into the deep pelvis and stitched below the vaginal closure and anastomosis to prevent potential fistula formation.

The surgeon meticulously sutures the common aneurotomy, ensuring complete closure by tying two sutures together. The original posterior suture is extended to form an outer anterior row, fully imbricating the anastomosis. A proctoscopy confirms the tightness of the anastomotic lumen, allowing passage of a nineteen millimeter proctoscope without obstruction. A leak test is performed, showing satisfactory results. Pericolic fat is placed over the exposed bone, and omentum is stitched below the vaginal closure and anastomosis to prevent potential fistula formation.
